text: One person that made me feel the magic of coding. And I remember him going like 'Ajke ekta bombastic problem solve korbo'

In terms of teaching he's the GOAT without any doubt. But apart from the teaching part no one can match the gem of a human being sir is. Sir is such a kind soul that when his students didn't do well in an exam sir blames himself and tell 'maybe I did lack in teaching you guys'. But in reality it was fault of the students because we didn't study properly.
KING! GOAT
GOAT PURAI

The best human, mentor, teacher you will ever find in your BracU journey. The man with zero hater! Always ready to help you. You are unlucky if you don't get the chance to meet him & attend his class.
GOAT of all GOATS
Best CSE110 faculty
He is one of the best faculty in BRACU I have ever seen, he is a good human being also, he gives many instruction, which as a freshers I need most.
If you follow his lecture notes closely and ask questions upfront, his insights are genuinely deep. Very approachable on Discord — always responsive.
Moves too fast and relies heavily on slides without simplifying complex logic. Great personality outside class, but classroom delivery needs work.
Polarizing faculty. Brilliant person and super available, but teaching clarity is inconsistent. Works best if you are a self-learner.
Quizzes feel unnecessarily difficult and pacing is chaotic. Outside class he's great — Discord availability is 10/10. High variance experience.
Use his consultation hours and stay on top of the material and it becomes manageable. Not for passive learners at all.
Recent semesters have dropped significantly. Students from 230 are saying run — I would echo that. His past reputation does not reflect the current experience.
One of the hardest courses made harder by fast-paced teaching. Marking is inconsistent — can be lenient one exam, CG crusher the next.
Course notes are dense but valuable. His Discord support is the only reason I passed. Would not take again but respect the effort he puts in outside class.
Generally a good experience. Quizzes are easy but finals are a different beast. Build a good rapport early and she is very supportive.
Teaching is monotonous and speech-heavy. Clear favouritism in marking — if she likes you, grade bumps happen. If not, expect a tough time.
She was genuinely great with me — supportive, fair marks, and always willing to help in labs. Course load is very manageable.
High-risk high-reward. Her class is a split experience — some students rave, some walk away feeling targeted. Stay on her good side.
Labs are fine and she genuinely helps out during sessions. Lectures can drag but you cover what you need. Watch for inconsistency in marking.
Described as angel by some and CG destroyer by others — both are accurate depending on which side you fall on. Average teaching, politics-heavy marking.
Reads directly from slides and has a weak grasp of advanced Java. Her notes and past papers are excellent but class attendance barely helps.
Mocked a student for asking a question during consultation. Behavior is unacceptable. Below-average teaching. The only saving grace is the past paper collection.
Once praised as goated and pookie, but the last two semesters have been a completely different person. Volatile, mocking, and unpredictable. Proceed with extreme caution.
Run fly or die — the warning is real. Her resources are the best in the department for CSE220 but the emotional toll of her behavior is not worth it for most students.
For a first programming course, clarity is everything and she does not deliver it. Uses ChatGPT-generated code examples sometimes which confuses more than it helps.
Behavior is the biggest concern. Known to be dismissive of female students and harsh to anyone she perceives as not trying. Not a comfortable learning environment.
bro FFR is literally GOAT tier for CSE250. quizzes straight from slides, marking is insane, replies on discord at 2am ngl. 11/10 no cap
took FFR for 250 and never looked back. explains theory before jumping to circuits unlike most. assignments easy. the type of teacher who actually gives a damn. highly recommend
FFR is a gem fr. marking is so generous you'll be shocked. turned CSE250 from a nightmare into something enjoyable for me. angel behavior absolute 10/10
RAO's handnotes are literally all you need. covers everything from zero. quizzes are 4 questions where only 3 count so you get a free miss. 10/10 would take again
doesn't care if final answer is wrong, gives marks for correct steps. very partial mark friendly. dude is passionate about circuits ngl. GOAT fr
got me from failing midterm vibes to a solid A. his notes + discord availability is elite. never makes you feel stupid for asking dumb questions. top tier
TAV records his own videos for students lol who does that?? absolute pookie fr. bonus marks throughout semester literally saved my cgpa
asked him the same question 3 times bc i kept forgetting and he explained every time without getting annoyed. that's TAV for you. GOAT behavior 10/10
marking is so generous bro. wrote half blank on one question and still got marks somehow. bonus marks on top of that. script checking is a vibe ngl
AKDB's marking is just insane. students call it 100/10 and honestly that tracks. bonus ungraded assignments for extra marks while you actually learn. lab vivas no stress at all
first quiz was rough bc of time pressure but he fixed that in later ones. patient, answers on discord if you cant make morning consultation. solid choice for 250
been a lab fav for years. no bias, chill guy, personally calls students to check if they understood the material. CSE250 under AKDB was genuinely stress free
SDS is a teaching boss. 10/10 delivery, covers everything, no rushing. follows rubrics strictly so don't expect free marks. fair is fair. 4.0 is possible if you actually attend
one of the genuinely good human beings at bracu. treats you like a person not an attendance number. explains circuits in a way that just clicks. marking is honest no games
calling him legend is underselling it fr. strict marking but completely fair, partial marks where deserved. pookie behavior. one of the best in cse dep no cap
AQT is pookie and best of best no cap. lab vivas are stress free with her. marking is very lenient compared to stricter faculty for CSE250. behavior absolute 10/10
some say her theory lectures are average but honestly her marking makes up for everything. super comfortable to approach. one of the most student friendly in the cse dep
took CSE250 under AQT and zero regrets. doesn't make you feel stupid for basic questions. low pressure throughout. marking is lenient and fair tbh
NFS aka Need for Speed lol. pookie faculty fr. best in lab settings, patient with all queries, never makes you feel bad for not knowing something. highly recommend
Nafis Faisal is just a good guy. grading is fair, doesn't penalize for no reason, easy to talk to. been recommended by students for multiple semesters for a reason
best thing about NFS is the zero stress environment. explains clearly, patient with confusion, humble vibes. 250 and 350 under him was a good experience ngl
technically solid, hand notes are detailed. but bro asks random questions mid class and WILL scold you if you can't answer. not for everyone. high risk section
mixed experience honestly. speaks softly so hard to follow. no script review so you never know where you lost marks. proceed with caution fr
expects perfect calculations, little to no partial credit if final answer is wrong. strict classroom vibe. watch youtube before class or you'll struggle ngl
niaz sir is genuinely the pookie of CSE ngl. class atmosphere is zero stress, explains stuff without making you feel dumb. underrated gem fr
his marking is actually lenient af. even if your logic is like 10% correct he still throws partial credit. bro literally saved my cgpa
dont expect grade bumps at semester end tho, he says marks are already generous and tbh he's not wrong. fair guy
behavior top tier. sweetheart, approachable, giving QUZA vibes no cap. only thing is class can feel slow if you zone out so stay attentive
if you want a chill stress free CSE101 section pick NZRF blindfolded. gentleman mentor energy
ngl TNMF for theory is rough. lectures feel dry and half the info doesnt even match what comes in exams. got blindsided in finals after doing fine in mids
marking is pathetic fr. no partial, no grace marks, script recheck is a lost cause. inconsistent af too
she seems sweet in person sometimes but after finals good luck getting a mail reply. dismissive during script consultation too
lab section is kinda okay with her, but theory is a straight up run. foundation courses under her hit different and not in a good way
sabbir sir is a red flag for CSE101 bro. lectures feel like he is forced to teach. 3/10 teaching is being nice honestly
bro literally cant debug our code during consultation. we end up learning from batchmates and youtube recordings instead
always late to class we literally call him late boy. the dont care energy makes him super unapproachable for real
marking is decent-ish (like 7/10 maybe) but he's stingy with partial and projects get slashed without clear reason. no grade bumps either
nice person outside class maybe but for a cgpa sensitive course? risky pick fr. taking him is a gamble
Teaching is top-notch. He uses practical examples to make logic click — even confusing topics start to feel doable after a couple of classes.
Marking is genuinely generous. Got strong partial marks on quizzes where my logic was right but small things were off. Fair guy.
Honestly one of the kindest faculty in CSE. Patient, humble, never makes you feel stupid for asking basic questions in consultation.
Quizzes are standard to slightly hard but they prepare you really well for the midterm/final. Don't skip them.
Took CSE111 with him after CSE110 and it was equally good. Punctual, prepared, makes class engaging without being chaotic.
Funny warning: don't show up to consultation in Barcelona gear. He's a die-hard Real Madrid fan and the jokes never stop.
Strict about protocols (deadlines, formatting) but if you follow the rules everything is smooth. Never lost marks for stuff that wasn't my fault.
If you want a peaceful semester with good grades AND actually learn programming logic, take RKBM. No regrets.
Underrated faculty fr. Teaching is straight to the point — if you actually pay attention in class you don't even need YouTube tutorials.
Marking is fair and rubric-based but he's generous with partial credit. Got mark bumps during script viewing just by explaining my logic.
Took CSE111 with him too. Same vibe — efficient teaching, low-stress class, no GPA panic moments.
One important warning: zero tolerance for plagiarism. If you copy code in his section you will get caught and your grade will tank.
His CSE230 lectures are the clearest I've seen. Discrete math is hard but he breaks it down step by step.
Wish he solved a wider variety of edge-case problems but everything covered in class is gold. Quizzes follow the same patterns.
Behavior is genuinely 10/10. Polite, humble, never talks down to students. Approachable for grade-boundary discussions if you put in genuine effort.
Quizzes can be hard but they prepare you really well. By midterm you realize his quiz pattern is exactly what shows up on exams.
Legend. KNI builds your programming foundation from absolute zero with so much care. Tracing finally made sense in her class.
Behavior is angelic, like a second mom. Always kind, always patient, always punctual. Replies to emails fast.
Marking is strict but completely fair. You get exactly what you earned — no extra bonuses, no unfair deductions either.
BIG WARNING: quizzes start easy but get significantly harder after the mid-sem break. Don't get complacent in week 4.
Continued with her into CSE111. Same dedication, same fairness. If you actually want to learn programming and not just chase grades, take her.
Everyone I know who got A/A- in her section attended every single lecture. The ones who skipped struggled hard.
Coding ethics matter big time in her section. She catches plagiarism and won't tolerate it — easy way to ruin your semester.
Energetic and systematic. Live-codes everything in class. If you pay attention you actually learn programming, not just memorize syntax.
Step-by-step explanations are top-notch. He covers difficult codes that other faculty completely skip.
Marking is rule-based and fair. If your code works you get marks, if it doesn't you don't. Don't expect grace marks.
Real talk: he gets visibly annoyed if you're zoned out during lecture. Stay engaged or sit at the back quietly.
Quizzes are tricky on purpose — they're designed to prepare you for the midterm/final pressure. Practice his class files with a timer.
The people who did well in his section just took his words like gospel. If he says practice X, do it. It pays off in finals.
Friendly and humble outside class. The strict vibe is only when teaching — he wants you to actually get it.
Very student-friendly. Lucid voice, gentle teaching style. Foundation-friendly for CSE110 if you also do some self-study on the side.
Marking is the highlight. She's lenient, accepts late assignments without penalty in many cases. Generous on internal stuff.
Made hardware concepts in CSE230 actually digestible for me. Some find her style slow but I appreciated it.
Pre-exam review classes are gold. Her suggestions overlap heavily with actual questions — don't skip them.
CSE320 with her was the chillest semester. Behaved like a sweet older sister, very approachable for any concept doubts.
Slight warning: midterms/finals are strict-rubric. She doesn't bump grades by more than 1 mark even if you ask nicely.
Behavior is 10/10. Sweet, well-mannered, never raises voice. Just don't side-talk in class — that's her one trigger.
Heads up: punctuality and quiz mark release can be slow. You might not know your standing till close to finals.
Highly organized teaching. He doesn't just follow slides — he gives deeper insights that build your foundation properly.
Beginners can find his class overwhelming because of volume. If you're not committing time it'll feel like drinking from a firehose.
Marking has gotten stricter recently. He doesn't give grace marks unless you can actually defend your logic in script viewing.
If you want to actually master Data Structures, take AIB. His insights on recursion and trees are unmatched in the dept.
He values logic over effort. Wrong code = no marks even if you wrote 200 lines. Right logic with bugs = good partial.
Most student-friendly faculty in CSE no debate. Replies to emails at 1AM. Will help with internship references if you ask.
Mentor-level outside class. Spend 15 mins in consultation and you come out with career advice + course advice + life advice.
Not beginner-friendly — he assumes you already have some coding base. If you're starting from zero you'll need YouTube alongside his lectures.
Marking is the best part. Generous, bonus opportunities in quizzes, considers grace marks if you're at a grade boundary.
His explanations skip a lot of intermediate steps. Experienced coders will be fine, beginners struggle.
For CSE422L he's actually solid — at upper level his style works because everyone has the prereqs. Plus marking carries hard.
Best for research guidance and academic advice. If you want a thesis supervisor or internship reference, he's your guy.
Behavior is solid — supportive, humble, treats students with respect. Some complaints about phone use in class but overall a favorite.
Approachable on Slack/Discord for non-class stuff. Career advice is genuine and detailed.
Teaching is decent for building basics but some recent semesters had explanation errors. Verify with classmates.
Major issue: script viewing and mark updates can be massively delayed. Don't expect to know your standing until late.
Marking is inconsistent. Some friends got generous bumps, others got strict deductions on similar work. Lottery vibes.
Don't bother messaging him on Discord or email. He often doesn't reply. You'll have to catch him after class for any clarification.
On the plus side: very flexible with assignment deadlines. Can extend if you ask. Quiz delays for genuine reasons usually accepted.
Behavior is mood-dependent. Some classes he's friendly and engaging, other days dismissive. Pretty unpredictable.
Reviews from older semesters are way better than recent ones. Something has shifted — be aware before registering.
Teaching is genuinely good — strong technical knowledge, detailed explanations. But class is theory-heavy with not enough hands-on coding.
Marking is the BIG PROBLEM. Small mistakes lead to massive deductions. Got close to zero on a quiz where my logic was 90% right.
Only flawless, clean code gets full marks. Even slightly messy logic = significant deduction. Be perfect or be punished.
Marks come back super late, sometimes close to finals. You go into the final not knowing where you stand. Brutal for anxiety.
Don't bother asking on Slack. Most messages get ignored. You're on your own for clarifications.
Behavior is unpredictable — some days he's helpful in class, others he's distant. Hard to read what mood you'll get.
If you want to survive: don't depend on his lectures. Build your own routine — YouTube, daily coding practice, timed mock quizzes.
Friends who got decent grades all said the same thing: stop expecting feedback during the semester and grind on your own.
Teaching is way too advanced for CSE110. She introduces complex logic into simple problems and beginners get lost fast.
Fast-paced lectures. If you blink you miss something. Note everything down or you'll fall behind in week 1.
Same problem in CSE111 — assumes you have CSE110 concepts down cold. Average students struggle.
Communication is rough. Class cancellations announced last minute, replies to messages can be slow.
For higher-level courses like CSE331 her style works better — seniors can keep up with her pace.
Quizzes are difficult but they do prepare you for finals. Survive the quiz pain and the actual exam feels familiar.
Teaching for CSE110 was uninspired. Felt like he didn't want to be there. Most of us learned from RKBM/KNI recordings instead.
He literally couldn't debug our consultation code last semester. We brought a problem and he stared at it. Awkward.
Marking is the only saving grace — fairly generous. But the lectures are a waste of time, just attend for attendance.
Risky pick for CSE110 if you actually want to LEARN programming. Choose another section if you can.
Personally a nice person but professionally underwhelming. CGPA-sensitive students should avoid.
sir is great
Simply the best in every regards
Teaching is frequently praised for breaking down topics from the absolute basic level, ensuring everyone from the front to the back of the class understands the logic.
Marking is very fair and often described as lenient, with many students noting that he naturally considers student effort and often rounds up marks without being asked.
He is known for his calm and patient behavior, always listening to individual problems and solving them cordially during or after class.
His sections are among the most sought-after in the university, often filling up instantly during the earliest stages of advising.
Students who managed to secure a 4.0 typically stayed consistent with class lectures because his explanations are often clearer than online resources.
Multiple reviews suggest that those who did well focused on following his guidance and logic patterns, which makes quizzes and exams feel much more manageable.
Teaching is top notch and passionate. Students describe him as one of the ultimate GOATs and gems of the CSE department.
He solves every code manually on the board rather than relying on slides or pre-written scripts, helping students understand the underlying logic.
His tracing tricks for CSE111 and CSE221 are famous for saving students' grades.
He provides high-quality YouTube videos and playlists for every chapter, widely considered a lifeline for the student body.
Marking is consistently described as top and lenient, and students report getting more than they deserve under his evaluation.
He gives bonus questions in quizzes, adding to the final total and helping students secure a 4.0.
Behavior is flawless; students call him an absolute gem, sweetheart, and friendly like a friend.
He is highly responsive on Slack and genuinely empathetic to students' academic and personal struggles.
Students warn that he values honesty above all else; a common tip is to never lie to him under any circumstance.
MNP is considered a GOAT faculty, especially for CSE220 and CSE330, with students calling him an angel, gem, and pookie.
He is praised for clarity and for correcting students' fundamental misconceptions.
He is particularly effective in lab settings, where he is hands-on and helpful.
A minor criticism is that he sometimes teaches quite fast and finishes classes early, using pictures of his handwritten notes as slides.
His quiz questions are generally easy if students follow his lectures.
He is one of the most generous markers in the CSE department and is known for being lenient and fair.
In CSE330, his marking is considered excellent, with many students achieving full marks in labs.
He is described as a king and a gentleman who treats every student with immense respect.
He is approachable, kind, and friendly, often going out of his way to help students during and after the semester.
Teaching is highly rated, especially for AI and CSE422.
He puts immense effort into lectures and explains complex topics like Neural Networks with great clarity.
For CSE220, most find his teaching great, though a few suggest self-studying the nuanced surface-level basics he covers.
Marking is nearly legendary, with students surprised by how generous he is and examples of fractional quiz marks being rounded up.
He is known for top-notch partial marking; if you write something relevant, you will be rewarded.
He proactively bumps grades for students stuck just 1-2 marks away from a higher letter grade.
He is described as the most gentle and well-behaved faculty who is incapable of getting angry.
He is a matir manush who inspires students with his calm and cool demeanor.
A small warning: he follows deadlines strictly for lab assignments, and missing one without a valid reason may lead to a firm no on late submissions.
Teaching is exceptional, concise, on-point, and backed by extensive resources and practice materials.
His classes are well-structured, making exam preparation straightforward.
In CSE330 and CSE221, he makes extra efforts to ensure every student understands the logic.
Most students rate his marking highly because he gives generous partial marks and has been known to give automatic 1-mark grade bumps.
Some newer reviews suggest he has become strictly rubric-bound and rarely gives free marks without merit.
Quizzes are generally considered easy and are based on class lectures.
Behavior is flawless; he is described as a gentleman and dedicated mentor who is highly responsive to student queries.
Teaching is elite; he is praised for clear, structured, concept-oriented explanations and for teaching the logic of Data Structures.
He prefers whiteboards and concepts over technology, and students say taking notes in his class creates a strong study guide.
He does not just teach Java; he teaches the logic of Data Structures, with easy ABC-style quizzes based on fundamentals taught in class.
Initially he was reported as generous with partial marks, but some students from his first semester found final marking blind-sided and average.
Behavior is exceptional; he is hardworking, dedicated, helpful, and puts in more effort than most regular faculties.
Teaching is his strongest suit, rated GOAT level by many, and he simplifies complex Data Structure concepts effectively.
He simplifies Numerical Methods effectively and is considered a fresh, high-energy teaching choice for CSE330.
In CSE220, his classes are described as OP level, breaking down logic in a way that is easy to grasp.
A few students mentioned he might occasionally struggle in lab environments compared to theory.
Marking is fair and standard; strict rubric followers call him strict, while others are happy because he gives partial credit for logic in the right direction.
His quizzes are easy to tricky but manageable if you attend lectures.
Behavior is top-tier; he is exceptionally friendly, helpful, and passionate, creating a positive classroom atmosphere.
Teaching is rated around 7/10; she is gentle, student-friendly, and a safe pick for anxious students.
In CSE330, students noted she struggles with mathematical derivations, often leading students to seek help from YouTube or other faculty notes.
Her handnotes and practice sheets are considered very helpful and sufficient for acing exams.
Marking is her strongest point, and students call her lenient with full marks possible if the process is shown.
In CSE220, she is known to be fair and may consider small grade bumps if class performance is consistent.
Behavior is flawless; she is too sweet, considerate, gentle-hearted, and patient in lab environments.
Teaching is organized and well-paced for beginners, but recent reviews suggest quality can feel moderate or below average depending on the course.
He is extremely thorough, sometimes treating classes like chocolates and creating a smooth end-of-semester experience.
Marking is debated: long-term reviews call him lenient and fair, but recent feedback labels him strict with zero partial marks in some sections.
He follows a strictly fair rubric, giving marks only when code is logically correct or when students can defend the answer during script viewing.
Behavior is top-tier, humble, funny, and gem-like, but students should not expect grade bumps just because he is nice.
Teaching quality is praised for thorough explanations and deep technical knowledge, though classes can feel theory-heavy without enough hands-on coding practice.
Marking is notoriously strict with very little partial credit; small mistakes or messy code can lead to massive mark cuts.
He is often late to classes, irregular with returning quiz and exam marks, and may show unpredictable mood swings between supportive and cold.
The biggest shock for students is not knowing marks until the end of the semester, making it hard to know whether they are doing okay.
Students who managed decent grades stopped depending on his classes and built their own routine using YouTube, online resources, and timed coding practice.
Teaching is generally rated mediocre, and she is criticized for reading from slides and sometimes failing to understand correct student logic.
Her biggest asset is her resource folder, which spoon-feeds code and is sufficient for self-study.
Marking is inconsistent: some students still find her lenient, while others report mass failures and horrible marking where minor logic errors result in zero.
She is strictly rubric-oriented now, though she may bump grades if she likes the student.
Behavior is her lowest point; she is described as arrogant, rude to selected students, and unprofessional even toward lab faculties.
A critical warning says she can be extremely harsh as an invigilator, with reports of expelling students for minor interactions or tearing up scripts.
Teaching is highly subjective: some find it top-notch and detail-oriented, while others label it hard to understand.
He is known to be slow in completing the syllabus but offers unique shortcuts for math-heavy courses like CSE330.
He emphasizes pseudocode and conceptual explanations over rote memorization.
Marking varies: some semesters have binary marking with little compromise, while others are lenient with generous partial marks.
He often avoids showing scripts before final grade submission.
Behavior is generally friendly and chill, though some students note professionalism or strictness issues around classroom attention.
He is praised for empathy during difficult times, including personally checking on students' well-being during the 2024 protests.
Teaching is great and mind-blowing; students say he puts maximum effort into making complex CSE221 and CSE230 topics understandable.
He is very active on Discord and offers extra online or in-person consultation slots if he misses a message.
Marking is often described as the best in BRAC, with students saying he actively looks for reasons to give marks rather than deduct them.
One student reported receiving 75% marks on a question where they would have given themselves zero, showing his generous partial marking style.
Behavior is described as infinity out of ten: pookie, humble, smiling, relatable, and welcoming like a big brother.
A practical warning from students is that his sections fill up almost instantly during advising, so he should be a top priority if available.
Teaching is a major strength; students describe him as exceptionally organized and concise, helping them grasp fundamentals quickly.
He is a favorite for heavy core courses like CSE220 and CSE422, and students say effort matters more than the faculty's guidance being lacking.
Marking is fair and gentle; he is known to mark gently, update marks regularly, and sometimes provide grade bumps up to 1.5 marks.
In lab sessions he is known for asking easy, basic-level viva questions that help students score.
Some students find his lecture style less engaging for software engineering topics, but most still consider him reliable and organized.
Behavior is exemplary; students call him pookie, humble, and cooperative, though advising feedback can be more mixed.
Teaching is generally rated excellent and highly dedicated, especially for making complex CSE320 architecture topics manageable.
For CSE221, reviews are more mixed: some appreciate her structured approach, while others find the slide-reading style lacking for time complexity topics.
She often takes extra review classes before midterms and finals, which students find highly beneficial.
Marking is fair to good: she follows rubrics more strictly now but still gives bonus marks or partial marks when the logic is sound.
Behavior is her defining strength; students describe her as an absolute sweetheart who gives life advice and treats students kindly regardless of grades.
A critical warning is that her quizzes can be lengthy and time-consuming, so speed is essential.
Teaching is top-notch and goated; students say he explains every detail with clarity and deep academic rigor.
If you attend his lectures and take notes, students say you likely will not need to study from books.
For CSE320, he is slow and thorough, often holding extra online classes to complete the syllabus.
Marking is controversial: students describe it as binary or standard-strict, with no grace marks or unsolicited requests tolerated.
He values industry standards, so partial marks are rare unless the logical process is significantly correct.
Behavior is sophisticated and professional; students describe him as formal, soft-spoken, and helpful during consultation.
A critical warning is that he dislikes section swaps and conducts quizzes with strict seating arrangements similar to midterms.
He is highly organized and provides excellent resources and concise slides.
Teaching is detailed and covers basics thoroughly, though some students feel lectures can become lengthy or repetitive.
Marking follows a strict rubric; students generally get marks based on effort and receive exactly what they deserve.
He is approachable and empathetic, often helping students with personal struggles and academic advice.
His quizzes are generally considered easy and designed around class discussions and slides.
Some students say his personal stories and struggle comparisons can polarize opinions, even though the overall experience is organized.
His teaching style is polarizing: repeated simple concepts and detailed code walkthroughs help some students solidify basics, while others find it boring.
Marking is generally considered lenient and generous, with many students reporting an easy A plus or 4.0 due to fair evaluation and helpful quiz standards.
A major caution exists for CSE221: recent reviews claim he appeared underprepared or inexperienced, with harsher marking than in his CSE111 sections.
Behavior is consistently rated excellent, though there are isolated complaints about strict invigilation.
Students mention a 50 problems bonus mark system, though it usually bumps quiz marks rather than final grades.
Teaching is rated poorly; students often say he only understands what he teaches himself and gives confusing explanations for lab problems.
In CSE330, he is known to be very strict with time and not allow a single extra second during evaluations.
Students advise practicing coding beforehand and completing lab notebooks the night before to survive his class.
His marking is widely feared, with many students reporting a massive CGPA drop due to lab evaluations and very low lab final or viva scores.
Even when theory marks are good, students warn that his lab marking can pull down the overall grade.
Recent automated checkers may reduce the impact on core coding marks, but students say his personal assessment remains strict.
Behavior is criticized as lacking empathy; students have complained that he can be unresponsive to questions and email communication.
He is known to go to great lengths to catch plagiarism and make sure students understand every line of their code during vivas.
His teaching is very good, with a loud and clear voice, strong resources, and consistent effort to make every student understand the topic.
Teaching is exceptionally clear and thorough, especially for difficult architecture topics.
He breaks complex Digital Logic and Architecture material into digestible segments that make the theory feel manageable.
Students say he covers topics clearly without leaving gaps, and his marking is viewed as fair and logical-process friendly.
Behavior is a standout strength: approachable, humble, student-friendly, and helpful during consultations.
He sets a high academic standard, but students say he gives enough support for serious learners to reach it.
Teaching is solid and engaging; students usually rate his delivery highly.
He is known for using humor and relatability to make heavy CSE320 topics feel less boring.
Students appreciate his standard resources and say he is helpful when clearing doubts.
Marking is often described as very generous in midterms and assignments.
Some students warn that his final marking can be noticeably stricter than midterm marking.
He may bump a small amount of marks when he sees consistent effort.
Behavior is his strongest trait; students describe him as kind, funny, and friendly with students.
A practical warning is that his relaxed style can make students slack off, so discipline matters for CSE320.
Students describe her CSE320 teaching as excellent and dedicated, with strong patience for repeated explanations.
For CSE320, many students call her supportive and say she makes difficult architecture material manageable.
Recent reviews say her marking has become more fair and rubric-oriented compared with earlier very-lenient semesters.
Her behavior is consistently praised as kind, patient, and student-friendly.
Teaching style is divisive: some students find it caring and helpful, while others need self-study.
Students praise her for being able to answer complex networking and architecture questions instead of only reading slides.
Some students find her lectures surface-level or hard to follow, especially if they struggle with spoken delivery.
Students often recommend using outside playlists alongside her CSE320 classes to master the deeper material.
In CSE320 she is noted for being strong on specific math topics such as TDM and CSMA/CD.
Marking is considered her greatest strength, with generous partial marks when the procedure is correct.
Grade bumps are common enough that students mention them as a real advantage.
Behavior is widely praised: student-friendly, responsive, and encouraging during consultation.
A caution is that some students find her grading strict if the work is not relevant or up to a basic standard.
Teaching is usually described as okay to good, with some self-study still needed to master the curriculum.
He compensates for average delivery by providing comprehensive resources and materials that guide students well.

He may arrange makeup quizzes near the end of the semester for students who missed them.
A warning is that his extra-chill style can make students relax too much, so staying attentive is important.
Students describe her CSE320 teaching as clear and easy to understand.
Her lucid voice and calm delivery help simplify complex hardware and architecture topics.

She follows rubrics strictly for major exams and usually does not bump grades beyond a very small margin.

A practical concern is that punctuality and delayed mark uploads can leave students uncertain before finals.
Students say his CSE320 teaching is top-notch and deeply organized.
He explains every detail with clarity and expects students to take notes seriously.

Partial marks are rare unless the logical process is significantly correct.
Behavior is professional and sophisticated; students find him formal, soft-spoken, and helpful during consultation.
A warning is that he dislikes section swaps and can be strict about exam-style seating and rules.
Teaching is highly structured and systematic, and many students rate it very strongly.
His CSE320 YouTube playlist and resource pattern are repeatedly mentioned as major strengths.
Some students find him too fast or unclear in in-person lectures, especially in lab-style settings.
Students say sitting in front and staying engaged helps a lot because his foundation-building can be strong.

Partial marks are not guaranteed, so students need clear logical process rather than relying on generosity.

He is helpful with project-related problems and gives useful consultation support.
Students also mention that he is good for CSE424-style project help, which adds to his overall reputation.

Some find her lectures boring or monotonous, even though she is experienced and academic.

Students say she is fair in the sense that grades reflect exact work, but not lenient with grace marks.
Behavior is generally sweet and friendly, though she can be firm when students are not attentive.
She is known for giving helpful hints during quizzes to attentive students.
A warning is that very low enrollment in some CSE320 sections reinforces her high-stakes reputation for this course.

Students advise printing slides and adding class notes to stay on track.

Quiz and assignment difficulty can be strict, but final script checking may be more lenient than expected.

Some students have questioned his punctuality in previous semesters.
Teaching is absolute top-tier, with attractive and engaging lectures that often make external YouTube videos unnecessary.
He explains concepts so clearly that even difficult topics in CSE340 and CSE321 become manageable.
Some students note he could improve by showing more live simulations for CSE321.
Marking is generally fair and rubric-based, and recent reviews say he is generous despite tricky quizzes.
He is considered much better in marking than more rigid faculties, often giving more marks than students expect.
Behavior is extremely chill, professional, and friendly, creating a classroom where students feel alive and motivated.
He is highly responsive to students who follow his instructions and seek help when confused.
Teaching opinions vary from average to very poor, and some students find the classes difficult to engage with.
Some students find his lectures helpful when followed diligently, though self-study or external resources may still be needed.
His grading is celebrated as more than fair, making him a supportive choice for surviving CSE340.
He is recommended for students who struggled in prerequisite courses and need a supportive environment to clear CSE340.
Teaching is often described as difficult to catch or hasty, so classroom explanations may require follow-up questions.
He is exceptionally patient in one-on-one sessions, sometimes spending up to 1.5 hours explaining a single topic.
Marking is his strongest suit; students frequently receive more than expected and say he can increase marks when asked politely.
Behavior is chill and humble, though he now uses Google Forms for mark reviews to avoid direct confrontation.
He is protective of scripts and may discourage viewing them if a student already has a high score.

Her manual simulations of algorithms like CPU scheduling and page replacement help with long-term retention.
Her take-home quizzes are standard and help build exam confidence.
Marking is famously binary, so if the answer is not precisely what she wants, partial marks are rare.
She is fair in the sense that students get what they deserve, but she does not provide unearned grace marks beyond rare one-mark grade changes.
Lectures are generally effective and concept-oriented, though many students recommend following the PBK YouTube playlist for extra clarity.
He provides mock tests, though they may not mirror the actual midterm or final exams.

He is known to scale up grades significantly at the end of the semester and provide grade bumps for effort.
Personality is professional, friendly, and cooperative, often with interesting stories during class.
He is highly accessible during consultation hours and appreciates students who show genuine dedication to the course.
Teaching is rated low; her style is advanced and not beginner-friendly, often adding complex logic and extra variables to simple code.
She is fast-paced, so students are advised to note everything down to keep up.

Marking is widely described as strict, with very little partial credit if the logic is not perfect.
Her quizzes are notoriously difficult and grade bumping is almost nonexistent, even for students missing a higher grade by a fraction.
Behavior is mixed between sweet and unprofessional; students mention missed classes without notice and poor reactions to queries.
She explains complex circuit concepts in a very simple and understandable manner. Highly patient, ensuring all students' confusions are cleared during class or consultation.
Her provided soft notes are highly organized and are considered the primary resource for exam preparation. One of the best resources for CSE250.
Her marking is lenient and more than deserved. She provides significant partial marks if the working steps are shown correctly, even if the final calculation is slightly off.
Quiz questions are very easy, provided the student practices regularly. Students who get her section say they have won the lottery.

She is widely praised for being super helpful and well-behaved. She treats students with immense kindness and is highly responsive on Discord, making her one of the most approachable faculties in the university.

Described by students as a Pookie, Angel, and Sweetheart. She is known for her GOAT level soft notes and willingness to help students both during consultation hours and late at night on Discord. Top-tier recommendation for anyone taking CSE250.

SADA is the absolute GOAT for CSE340. His quizzes are easy, marking is insanely generous and he bumps grades at the end. Teaching is chill and low stress. 100% recommend if you need to survive this course.

Sir gives full marks on projects with minimum effort fr. The grace marks he gives at the end are unreal. Teaching is effective and he never makes things stressful. Hidden gem for CSE340 — sections fill fast for a reason.
SADA in CSE470 is an absolute blessing. Known as a wingless angel for a reason — his behavior is 10/10, marking is full, and quizzes are among the easiest I have seen. Highly recommended.

TWK is a boss in every sense. Will literally re-teach entire chapters in consultation if needed. His assignments are lengthy but they absolutely carry you in midterms and finals. Grace marks at end too. 10/10.

Man of pure heart no cap. After his lectures you genuinely don't need YouTube playlists. Super patient in consultations and will explain the same topic however many times you need. Marking is fair and lenient with plenty of partial marks.
TWK for CSE260 is a great experience. He explains everything in detail and never rushes. Responds fast to queries. Some deadlines are strict but overall a very supportive and goated faculty to have.

SWV's classes are so engaging it is literally impossible to not pay attention. Complex CSE340 topics become manageable under him. Quizzes can be tricky but marking is fair and even generous. Top tier faculty.

One of the most attractive teachers in the department. Explains CSE321 concepts so clearly you start asking questions out of curiosity not confusion. Very chill if you follow his instructions. Highly recommend.

No BS faculty who just wants you to learn. Lectures are top tier, external videos become unnecessary. Marking is very decent — students say they often get more than they deserved. Super chill to interact with.

RDW is the definition of super chill. Feels like your friend is teaching the class. Very responsive on DMs, extra consultation slots available. Partial marks are generous in math sections. Top tier choice for CSE340.

Pookie faculty fr. Goes out of his way to help even students not in his section. Explains things multiple times without getting frustrated. Assignments are submission-based so full marks if you just do it on time.

RDW in CSE461 is very good. A bit fast in pace for this higher level course but super helpful and willing to revisit topics. Partial credit on math questions is generous. Flexible attendance policy is a plus.

AFQ is a GOAT and Pookie. Teaching can be a bit dry but his marking more than compensates. Quiz questions are among the easiest and he gives grade bumps at end of semester. Behavior is absolutely 10/10.

Puts a lot of effort into teaching CSE260, generous partial credit in math sections. Some students find lectures hard to follow and rely on self-study but the marking is exceptional and very considerable overall.

Very chill and helpful faculty. If marking is your priority, AFQ is your guy — he is incredibly lenient and often gives extra marks beyond the rubric. Teaching style is not for everyone but overall solid choice.

SVKG's marking in CSE340 is genuinely 100/100. Called an angel by students for a reason. Quizzes and assignments are easy, midterm marking is very lenient. Teaching gets mixed reviews but grades are great.

If you struggled in prerequisite courses and need to clear CSE340, SVKG is your best option. His marking is stress free and incredibly fair. Teaching may not be the best but the supportive grading makes up for it.

SVKG teaches CSE321 with a kind and approachable demeanor. Behavior is absolutely top notch — he never breaks your spirit unlike stricter faculties. Teaching is average but marking is very generous and honest.

PBK is literally the King of 340. His YouTube playlists are all you need to survive the course. Super organized and punctual. Marking is fair and rubric based. Sections fill instantly because everyone wants him.

Top tier teaching no doubt. His assignments are long but they prepare you perfectly for midterms and finals. He is a perfectionist so do not ignore his guidelines or he will be strict. But overall a goated faculty.

PBK for CSE260 is a great experience. Thorough and organized teaching. Marking is decent and fair. He can be a bit stern if you ignore instructions but is very helpful and professional during consultations.
Awesome Teaching, but fair at marking

ZLNM is a genuine blessing for CSE423. Quizzes straight from slides, marking is incredibly generous with partial credit even if your final answer is wrong. Recent feedback says his teaching has improved a lot too.

GOAT for CSE340. BUET graduate so deep knowledge but also very approachable. Gives significant partial marks for working and steps even if the answer does not match. Makes a boring course engaging. Highly recommend.

Very good human being who genuinely cares about student success. Provides a stress-free environment. Early reviews said he was hard to follow but recent semesters he has been excellent. One of the best choices.

FFU is the king for CSE341. Makes difficult concepts manageable through repetition and brainstorming. Quizzes and assignments are easy. Generous partial marks. Has a Kabir Singh vibe but most agree it is just dedication not rudeness.

Goated faculty for 341. Very generous in marking and actively supports student grades. Can seem arrogant to some but most defend it as simple strictness. Design of his course makes tough content survivable.

FFU for CSE422 is solid. Dedicated, willing to explain topics multiple times. Maintains a clear teacher-student boundary that some may find intimidating but he is genuinely helpful for those who engage.

SU is very professional and finishes the syllabus on time. Explains hard topics on both slides and board. Quizzes can have tricky edge cases but they prep you for finals. Fair marking with partial marks. Solid choice.

Sumaiya for CSE341 is a great experience. Repeats complex topics until the class confirms understanding. Marking is fair and accuracy-based. Known to give small grade bumps at the end of the semester. Behavior is excellent.

SU covers everything diligently and is responsive to questions. Some find her style a bit dry but she uses mixed teaching mediums effectively. Fair marking, not super lenient but definitely not harsh either.
